title: Spec Building, Pre-Sales, Agent Relationships, and Material Selection
summary: Spec builders and custom home builders shared strategies around pre-sales versus completed spec listings, structuring relationships with real estate agents, and navigating client-supplied materials. The group also discussed product selection decisions including appliance packages, natural stone versus porcelain, and electrical considerations for high-end builds.

teachings.concept: Don't Let a Neighbor's Failure Dictate Your Decisions
teachings.explanation: When a nearby builder has a struggling project, it can skew your risk assessment unfairly. A poor result from a competitor is often due to their specific mistakes in location, design, or pricing—not a market-wide signal that your project will fail too.
teachings.quote: If somebody screwed up a project, it doesn't mean yours is going to be too.

teachings.concept: Right Location, Right House, Right Price
teachings.explanation: Three factors determine whether a property sells or sits: the location, the quality of architecture and design, and the price. Missing any one of these will stall a project regardless of market conditions.
teachings.quote: You've got to have the right location, the right house, meaning architecture, design, layouts, and the right price. And if you don't, it just sits there.

teachings.concept: Product Mix and Staggered Completions for Risk Mitigation
teachings.explanation: Holding a variety of projects at different price points and in different areas, while staggering their completion dates, reduces the risk of having too many unsold properties competing with each other at the same time.
teachings.quote: We like to have some low end, some higher end, a couple different areas, just a variety to mitigate the risk. And then we try to mitigate the risk to have them all finishing at the same time.

teachings.concept: The Art of the Possible for Pre-Sales
teachings.explanation: When selling proposed or custom construction, presenting a designed concept—even if everything is negotiable—gives buyers a visual and emotional starting point. This reduces the visualization gap that causes buyers to disengage from plans alone.
teachings.quote: Here's what I call the art of the possible. Do you have a hard time visualizing? Here's what's possible. But if this is not your style, we can turn it into anything you want.

teachings.concept: Listing Dirt and the Build Separately
teachings.explanation: A builder who is also a licensed agent must be careful about how they list land versus the construction contract, as some states (like Texas) require the brokerage to have ownership in the building company if they are affiliated. Separating land sales through a brokerage from build contracts through a building company can be a strategic and legal necessity.
teachings.quote: I'll sell the dirt, like for a custom home, I'll sell the dirt through my brokerage. But then the custom build will be through my building company and will not be affiliated with my brokerage.

teachings.concept: Buyer's Agent Has Thumb on the Scale
teachings.explanation: When a buyer is represented by an agent, that agent has significant influence over which property the buyer chooses. A builder needs a strong listing agent who can educate buyer's agents on the value of finishes and justify pricing, or risk losing deals to less expensive but more aggressively represented properties.
teachings.quote: An agent is working with a buyer. They've got a $60,000 commission on the line. So they're showing them houses that are done, complete for sale, and your house comes up. Agent's going to have the thumb on the scale of which direction the buyer goes.

teachings.explanation: Rather than estimating costs and then setting a price, experienced spec builders determine the maximum sellable price for a given lot first, then back into the allowable build budget. All design decisions are then shoehorned into that budget.
teachings.quote: I know what I can spend to build it. The budget's 2.5. And then we have to fit all the design stuff into it. I'm budget first.

teachings.concept: Client-Supplied Materials Create Risk Without Recourse
teachings.explanation: When clients purchase their own materials, the builder loses their markup income, their leverage with suppliers, and their ability to warranty or resolve problems. Problems with client-supplied items become the builder's operational headache without corresponding compensation.
teachings.quote: How many times can I just keep showing up for something that you ordered that I haven't charged you for?

actionable_advice: Add a clause to your contract stating that if a client backs out after a certain point, all time spent is charged at a defined hourly rate.
Add a clause to your contract stating that any client-supplied materials are still subject to your cost-plus markup to compensate for the coordination and risk involved.
When building a spec house that you want to pre-sell, create renderings of key spaces (entry, main living areas, outdoor areas) rather than relying on floor plans, which most buyers cannot read.
List a proposed build in two ways simultaneously: as a land listing with plans and renderings attached, and as a completed residential listing priced at the finished home value with renderings.

To find the best agent for new construction listings, contact the largest title company in your area and ask which agents consistently close the most new builds—not just recently, but consistently over time.

When co-brokering a custom home deal, structure agent compensation in two parts: a standard percentage on the land sale and a reduced percentage (e.g., 2%) on the build, with the agent actively managing the client relationship throughout construction.
For custom or remodel work, do not quote a price until all final selections have been made by the client, the interior designer, and the trades have signed off on all plans.

When upgrading electrical service beyond 200 amps for features like heated driveways, engage the utility company early—re-engineering the service lines is time-consuming and can constrain the entire project.
If you are building a spec house and a buyer wants to make changes mid-build, charge them for all completed work to date plus the cost of changes plus your full markup percentage.
Consult your title company to get plugged into local builder networking groups where off-market properties and agent relationships are cultivated.
client_challenges: A builder has two lots available and is uncertain whether to build spec homes or list them as proposed/custom construction pre-sales, especially with a neighboring builder's house sitting unsold for a year.

A builder has had to pivot their business model multiple times in four years as margins at middle price points have been squeezed by rising costs and regulations, making it difficult to determine which project types are worth pursuing.
A builder put a cancellation fee clause in their contract but is conflicted about whether to actually enforce it when clients back out after significant time has been invested.
A builder is dealing with a client who ordered $60,000 worth of plumbing fixtures themselves, and problems with those fixtures are becoming the builder's operational burden without any compensation.

A client wants to source stone from Turkey through a vendor their friends have used, creating quality, warranty, and timeline risk for the builder and fabricator with no recourse if something goes wrong.
A spec builder has been sending plans to interested buyers and getting no response, struggling to convert interest into pre-sale contracts without renderings or a more compelling visual presentation.

A builder in a market with mostly infill construction has difficulty pricing new spec builds because there are no comparable new construction sales—custom homes in the area never list publicly and non-disclosure laws prevent accessing sale prices.
A builder is unsure whether to use natural stone tile ($35/sq ft) versus a similar porcelain ($8/sq ft) in the entry and mudroom of a $4 million spec home.
notable_quotes: On competitor failure: 'If somebody screwed up a project, it doesn't mean yours is going to be too.'

On pre-sale visualization: 'Here's what I call the art of the possible. Do you have a hard time visualizing? Here's what's possible. But if this is not your style, we can turn it into anything you want.'
On agent influence: 'Agent's going to have the thumb on the scale of which direction the buyer goes.'
On appliance brand recognition: 'I go to sell the house and nobody knew that I put $20,000. Like, I might as well put $5,000 appliances. It's the name.'
On holding costs: 'That's what kills a builder. And I know that from the inside, you need to find an agent that can work with you on that.'
On timeless design: 'I've never been to a European city and been like, gosh, this is so ugly.'
